title: Red Wine on Leather
stainChemistry: Classification: Tannin (polyphenolic). Red wine contains anthocyanin pigments (pH-dependent color: red at acidic, blue at alkaline, colorless when reduced), condensed tannins, and ethanol. Anthocyanins bond strongly to protein and cellulose fibers. The flavylium cation (red form) is the primary staining species. On leather (pigmented/finished): leather requires gentle, pH-neutral treatment. CRITICAL: Never use H₂O₂, sodium percarbonate, or any oxidizer on leather — permanently strips finish, dye, and natural oils. Use sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach) for color removal instead.
whyThisWorks: Red wine gets its deep color from grape skin pigments that bond to surfaces and darken with air exposure. Leather has a protective finish that resists most spills, but red wine penetrates through wear spots. Gentle leather-safe cleaning followed by conditioning protects the hide and replaces the natural oils lost during treatment.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin and tamp gently. This acidic spotter targets the bonds that wine, tea, and fruit juice pigments form with the surface. Work from the edge toward the center. Check progress every few minutes. Dwell 5–10 minutes.

2. Apply sodium hydrosulfite solution to the stain. Monitor carefully — check every 2–3 minutes for color change. This reducing agent is safe on leather and does not oxidize or strip the finish, dye, or natural oils the way peroxide does. Do NOT follow with oxidizing agents without thorough flushing first. Dwell 5–10 minutes.
3. Flush with NSD and a mild leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin pigment and any excess reducing agent. Rinse thoroughly. Dwell 2–3 minutes.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to stop any remaining activity and restore the surface to its natural state. Blot dry and allow to air dry completely. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — no heat; heat dries out leather and causes cracking
warnings: Using household cleaners not designed for leather — strips finish
Using excess water — causes water spots and stiffening
Not conditioning after cleaning — leads to cracking

steps: 1. Dab any wet residue immediately with a clean white cloth. Do NOT rub.
2. Mix 1–2 drops of Dawn NSD into 1 cup of lukewarm water. Dampen a clean cloth — wring it out so it's barely damp, not dripping.
3. Gently dab and wipe the stained area from the outside inward. Do NOT scrub or soak the leather.
4. Wipe with a clean cloth dampened with plain water to remove soap residue. Immediately dry with a clean, dry cloth.
5. Apply a leather conditioner after the area is fully dry to restore moisture stripped during cleaning.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two gentle attempts, take it to a professional leather care specialist.
warnings: Always condition leather after any cleaning — cleaning strips natural oils.
Use a clean, soft cloth — rough materials scratch the leather finish.
Never use bleach, H₂O₂, or undiluted alcohol on leather.

neverDo: Use acetone, bleach, H₂O₂, or any oxidizer — strips finish and dye permanently
Use excess water — causes warping and spotting
Skip conditioning after cleaning — leads to cracking
fiberSensitivities: Leather: avoid bleach and all oxidizers — they strip color and finish. Always condition after cleaning. Test product on inconspicuous area first; leather varies greatly in dye stability. Avoid extended water saturation — causes warping and spotting.

escalation.when: Stain has penetrated past the protective topcoat, or home treatment leaves residual discoloration
escalation.whatToTell: Red wine stain on leather, tannin-based (anthocyanin pigments). Treated at home with mild soap and conditioner. Stain may have penetrated the finish. May require re-dyeing or professional restoration.

contextQuestions: Is the leather finished/pigmented or aniline/unfinished?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting and drying?
Has any product already been applied to the stain?
Is this a garment, handbag, furniture, or car interior?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Unfinished/aniline leather is far more porous and vulnerable — even water can leave marks. Fresh stains have much better outcomes. Car interior leather often has heavy topcoat that provides more protection. Any prior product application may have set the stain or damaged the finish.
